ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for multigrain rice; closest defensible parent is cooked rice (FOODON_03301710). Multigrain variant is a compositional extension of this class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Rice, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for multigrain or mixed-grain rice preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for multigrain rice as a distinct food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of multigrain rice weighs approximately 200 g cooked, providing around 280 kcal and 58 g of carbohydrates per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cooked Brown Rice (FDC ID 168878), Cooked Barley (FDC ID 170283), Cooked Millet (FDC ID 169702), and Cooked Black Rice composite
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Korean-style multigrain rice (japgokbap). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods entries for cooked brown rice (FDC ID 168878), cooked barley (FDC ID 170283), cooked millet (FDC ID 169702), and cooked black rice, weighted to reflect a typical Korean multigrain blend (approximately 40% brown rice, 20% barley, 20% black rice, 10% millet, 10% white rice). Energy 140 kcal/100g, protein 3.25 g, fat 1.0 g, carbohydrate 29.0 g, dietary fibre 2.0 g, sodium 2.5 mg, sugar 0.25 g, and saturated fat 0.2 g are consistent with this USDA composite. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and retained where consistent. USDA FoodData Central is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for Korean japgokbap (multigrain rice). GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous approved-source entries: mixed-grain rice and barley-enriched rice preparations reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) American Journal of Clinical Nutrition and the University of Sydney GI Database, which document GI values of 48–62 for barley-brown rice blends. A midpoint of 55 is used as a conservative low-GI estimate (GI ≤55 is classified as low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 58.0) / 100 = 31.9 / 2 = 15.95 per 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (2.5 mg/100g) with moderate dietary fibre and potassium (~85 mg/100g) supporting DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Multigrain rice is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its negligible sodium content (2.5 mg/100g); however, patients should avoid adding salt, soy sauce, or high-sodium condiments during preparation to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) and moderate carbohydrate load (29 g/100g); dietary fibre (2 g/100g) and beta-glucan from barley support glycaemic moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Multigrain rice has a lower glycaemic impact than white rice due to barley beta-glucan and whole-grain components; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to approximately 150–180 g cooked per meal and pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.2 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and whole-grain components (barley, brown rice, black rice) providing fibre and polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Multigrain rice is a heart-friendly staple with negligible saturated fat and no trans fat or cholesterol; the inclusion of barley and black rice provides beta-glucan and anthocyanins that may support LDL reduction and vascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate fibre and polyphenols (anthocyanins from black rice) present, but no direct omega-3 or strong anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Multigrain rice is not contraindicated for rheumatoid arthritis and provides modest anti-inflammatory polyphenols via black rice anthocyanins; however, it should be paired with omega-3-rich foods such as salmon or flaxseed to meaningfully support anti-inflammatory dietary goals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Multigrain rice (Korean: 잡곡밥, japgokbap) is a traditional Korean steamed grain dish made from a blend of whole grains including brown rice, black rice, barley, millet, and sometimes white rice. It is a staple in Korean cuisine, commonly served as the carbohydrate base alongside banchan (side dishes), soups, and main dishes such as bibimbap or grilled meats. The texture is slightly chewy and nutty compared to plain white rice, with a mild earthy flavour contributed by the barley and black rice. Nutritionally, multigrain rice provides approximately 140 kcal per 100 g cooked, with 3.25 g protein, 1.0 g fat, and 29.0 g carbohydrates. Its dietary fibre content of 2.0 g/100g and the beta-glucan from barley lower its overall glycaemic index to approximately 55 (low GI), making it a more glycaemically favourable choice than white rice. Sodium is negligible at 2.5 mg/100g, supporting its suitability for hypertension management. The anthocyanins in black rice and the whole-grain fibre contribute modest cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, and dairy-free, though it contains barley and is therefore not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten sensitivity. A standard serving is one bowl (200 g cooked), providing approximately 280 kcal and 58 g carbohydrates.
common_user_queries: Is multigrain rice healthier than white rice?
Is multigrain rice good for diabetics?
What grains are in Korean mixed grain rice?
How many carbs in multigrain rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite entries for the constituent grains (brown rice FDC 168878, barley FDC 170283, millet FDC 169702, black rice) as no single approved-source entry exists for Korean-style multigrain rice. Input values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and found consistent with the USDA composite. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2021). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a USDA FoodData Central composite adapted from cooked brown rice (FDC 168878), cooked barley (FDC 170283), cooked millet (FDC 169702), and black rice, weighted to a typical Korean japgokbap blend (approx. 40% brown rice, 20% barley, 20% black rice, 10% millet, 10% white rice). No single approved-source entry for japgokbap exists; USDA FoodData Central is the approved secondary global source used per Project Veritas v2 source policy. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — whole grain steamed rice blend contains no industrially produced trans fats per USDA FoodData Central reference entries for constituent grains). 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based grain dish containing no animal-derived ingredients). 4. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — cooked whole grains contain negligible vitamin C, confirmed by USDA FoodData Central). 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). 6. vitamin_d_mcg is reported as 0.0 (naturally zero — grains do not contain vitamin D). 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to -1: individual vitamin A RAE data for this specific multigrain blend are not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite entries consulted (FDC 168878, 170283, 169702) as of 2026-04-17; the constituent grains contain negligible preformed vitamin A and the composite value cannot be reliably derived. 8. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1: vitamin E data for this specific multigrain blend are not individually reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite entries consulted as of 2026-04-17; while individual grain entries contain small amounts, a reliable weighted composite value for this blend is not available. 9. selenium_mcg is set to -1: selenium data for this specific multigrain blend are not individually reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite entries consulted as of 2026-04-17; selenium content in grains varies significantly by soil origin and a reliable composite estimate is not available. 10. The dietary_flags gluten_free is set to false because barley is a gluten-containing grain; the input record's gluten_free tag has been corrected accordingly. 11. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on barley-brown rice blends in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021); direct GI testing of this specific blend has not been identified. 12. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.3 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.35 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profiles of the constituent grains. 13. foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type are set to 'closest_parent' as no exact ontology code exists for multigrain rice.
